narrative:
John Curtis has built a brand as the GOP's climate-conscious senator, founding the Conservative Climate Caucus to prove that Republicans can talk about the environment without actually doing anything about it. The caucus's guiding philosophy might be summed up as: "Climate change is real, and fossil fuels are the solution." The New Republic called the whole enterprise "nonsense," and environmental groups have pointed out that the caucus members collectively rake in millions from oil and gas interests. Curtis himself has received substantial fossil fuel industry contributions throughout his career, which makes his climate summits feel less like policy forums and more like industry appreciation dinners.

Then there's the stock trading. During the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020, Curtis made a series of suspiciously well-timed stock purchases, including buying shares of Abbott Laboratories around the time the company was receiving federal grants for COVID testing. Government watchdog Accountable.us filed a complaint with the Office of Congressional Ethics, though the office declined to investigate. Curtis later said the trades were made by a financial advisor, not him personally. To his credit, he divested from all individual stocks by December 2023 and says he supports banning congressional stock trading. But supporting something and actually getting it done are two very different things in Washington, and Curtis has been in government long enough to know the difference.

On the 80% Bill's twenty planks, Curtis is a study in selective populism. He'll talk a good game on Pentagon accountability and fiscal responsibility, but when it comes to taxing the ultra-wealthy, banning corporate PACs, or passing the DISCLOSE Act, the moderate mask slips. He sits on the Senate Commerce and Small Business committees — comfortable perches that keep him close to corporate interests while staying far from any legislation that might upset them. He voted to confirm every Trump cabinet pick, which tells you where the "moderate" label ends and the party loyalty begins.

Curtis replaced Mitt Romney, who at least had the occasional spine to break with his party. Curtis has shown no such inclination. He's the kind of senator who'll host a climate summit on Monday and vote against every piece of meaningful climate legislation on Tuesday. Utah deserves a senator who does more than rebrand the status quo with a green coat of paint.

Michael Cloud is the rare breed of congressman who actually supports banning stock trading for federal officials — and then proceeds to oppose virtually every other reform that would make government work for regular people. The Republican from Texas's 27th district, a former church media director turned Freedom Caucus warrior, has built his brand on fiscal austerity and small government. Translation: he will fight tooth and nail to make sure the Pentagon cannot account for trillions in assets, but heaven forbid we spend a dime helping working families.

Cloud introduced the bipartisan DIVEST Act alongside Democrat Jared Golden to ban stock trading by senior executive branch officials, declaring that "unelected bureaucrats who wield enormous influence" should not be trading stocks. A fine principle — except Cloud voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation that would save seniors billions. Every single House Republican voted no. Apparently, letting pharmaceutical companies charge whatever they want is the "small government" position.

As a Freedom Caucus board member and Appropriations Committee member, Cloud has positioned himself as a spending hawk. He was one of the 20 holdouts against Kevin McCarthy's speakership, leveraging the chaos for "institutional reforms." He talks tough about Pentagon waste — once suggesting defense spending should face scrutiny — but when it comes to actually auditing the Department of Defense, which has failed eight consecutive audits and cannot account for 63 percent of nearly $4 trillion in assets, Cloud's party has done nothing meaningful to force accountability. His district, which stretches along the Texas Gulf Coast from Corpus Christi to Victoria, is home to significant oil and gas operations, and the industry has rewarded him accordingly through campaign contributions tracked by OpenSecrets.

Cloud voted against certifying the 2020 electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania, aligning himself with the January 6th objectors. He voted against the CHIPS Act. He opposed continuing resolutions to keep the government funded. On paid family leave, junk fee bans, right to repair, corporate home buying restrictions, and the DISCLOSE Act — the silence is deafening. These are policies supported by roughly 80 percent of Americans, but Cloud is too busy cutting government spending on things people actually need to notice.

The man who wants to ban bureaucrats from trading stocks apparently draws the line at banning the corporate PAC money that flows into his own campaign. Fiscal responsibility for thee, but not for me.

Andrew S. Clyde has represented Georgia's 9th Congressional District since 2021, arriving in Washington with exactly the resume you would design if you wanted to illustrate the concept of conflict of interest: he is the owner of Clyde Armory, a gun store in Athens, Georgia, worth between five and twenty-five million dollars that earned him between one and five million dollars in income in 2020 alone [1]. A Navy veteran who grew a hobby business in his garage into a twelve-million-dollar-a-year firearms emporium, Clyde largely self-funded his first campaign with $740,000 of his own money [2]. He sits on the House Appropriations Committee and the Budget Committee, where he has used his position to advocate for abolishing the IRS and the ATF -- agencies that, by pure coincidence, regulate the tax obligations and firearms sales of businesses exactly like his own [3]. He is perhaps best remembered nationally for describing the January 6th Capitol attack as a "normal tourist visit," a characterization vividly contradicted by photographs showing Clyde himself, mouth agape, helping to barricade the House chamber doors against the mob [4].

On the twenty issues of the 80% Bill, Clyde's record is remarkably consistent -- consistently opposed. He has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, ending dark money, requiring the DISCLOSE Act, banning corporate PACs, or imposing a lifetime lobbying ban on former members. He voted against the For the People Act and its anti-gerrymandering and campaign finance reforms [5]. He voted against the PACT Act, which expanded VA benefits for veterans exposed to toxic chemicals -- a striking vote for someone who campaigns heavily on his Navy credentials [6]. On taxing the ultra-wealthy and closing the carried interest loophole, Clyde's position is the mirror image of his FairTax Act proposal: he wants to abolish the income tax entirely and replace it with a national sales tax, a plan that would shift the tax burden dramatically from the wealthy to working families [7]. He has not championed Medicare drug negotiation, paid family leave, right to repair, junk fee bans, or any of the kitchen-table economic provisions that poll above eighty percent with American voters.

The ethics of Clyde's legislative career are, to put it gently, structurally compromised. Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington filed a formal ethics complaint alleging that Clyde violated House rules by sponsoring and advocating for legislation -- including bills to reverse ATF restrictions on pistol braces -- that would directly benefit Clyde Armory's inventory and bottom line [8]. His financial disclosures show he earned between $1.25 million and $7.1 million from the store in 2021 and 2022, the same years he was pushing legislation to roll back firearms regulations [9]. He also distributed AR-15 lapel pins to fellow Republican members of Congress, turning the weapon into a fashion accessory for the legislative branch [10]. Meanwhile, he has introduced legislation to defund the ATF and block its enforcement actions -- the very agency that has been monitoring his gun store for potential compliance issues [11].

Clyde's campaign finance profile is unusual among his peers in that he relies less on PAC money and more on self-funding and individual contributions from the firearms and defense communities [12]. But the conflict runs deeper than donor lists: Clyde is not merely funded by the gun industry -- he is the gun industry, personally profiting from every deregulatory bill he introduces. On the Pentagon audit, he sits on Appropriations and votes for defense spending without demanding the audit accountability that the Department of Defense has failed to achieve for six consecutive years. On releasing the Epstein files, protecting farmland, banning corporate purchases of single-family homes, and funding Social Security, Clyde has been silent -- a silence that speaks as loudly as his AR-15 pin.

The bottom line on Andrew Clyde is not complicated: he is a firearms dealer who went to Congress and kept selling. The only difference is that now the product is legislation, and the customer is his own balance sheet.

Sean Casten represents Illinois's 6th Congressional District, a formerly reliable Republican suburban seat west of Chicago that he flipped in 2018 by running as a clean-energy entrepreneur who would bring scientific rigor to Congress [1]. Before politics, Casten spent two decades building businesses that turned waste methane into usable energy, a background he cites approximately every ninety seconds in any given interview [2]. He serves on the Financial Services Committee, which puts him squarely in the path of some of the 80% Bill's most consequential proposals.

To his credit, Casten has been one of the louder voices in the House on banning congressional stock trading. He cosponsored the TRUST in Congress Act and the Ban Conflicted Trading Act, arguing that lawmakers with access to nonpublic briefings have no business picking stocks [3]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which included the Medicare drug price negotiation provisions that let the government haggle on behalf of seniors for the first time [4]. He has cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act to drag dark money into the sunlight [5], backed the Junk Fee Prevention Act [6], and supported the FAMILY Act for paid family leave [7]. On ending forever wars, Casten voted to repeal the 2002 Iraq AUMF in 2021, a modest but real step toward reclaiming Congress's war powers [8].

Where Casten gets quieter is on the structural reforms that would limit the influence of the donor class he depends on. He has not cosponsored legislation for a lifetime lobbying ban, congressional term limits, or a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United, despite these being some of the most popular proposals in the 80% Bill. On auditing the Pentagon, a measure that enjoys broad public support, Casten has not been a notable champion, even as the Department of Defense has failed every single audit it has ever attempted [9]. He has also been largely silent on protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases and banning corporate buyers from snapping up single-family homes.

Follow the money and the silences start to make sense. In the 2024 cycle, Casten raised approximately $4.8 million, with his top industry donors including securities and investment firms, real estate interests, and leadership PACs from fellow Democrats [10]. The financial sector's generosity is particularly notable given Casten's seat on the Financial Services Committee, the very panel that oversees Wall Street regulation, carried interest tax rules, and housing policy [11]. He has declined to take corporate PAC money in his campaigns, which is genuinely notable, but leadership PACs and bundled contributions from industry executives can accomplish much the same work without the label [10].

Casten is the kind of Democrat who checks many of the popular boxes — drug pricing, stock trading bans, dark money disclosure — while carefully avoiding the reforms that would fundamentally restructure how power and money flow through Washington. His clean-energy backstory gives him a compelling brand, but his record suggests a legislator who knows exactly which fights are safe to pick and which ones might upset the donors who keep the lights on in a perpetually competitive district.

Ben Cline has built a brand as a fiscal warrior for the Shenandoah Valley, racking up a 97 percent lifetime score from Heritage Action while sitting on the Appropriations Committee — the very body that decides how to spend the money he performatively refuses to authorize. A proud member of the Freedom Caucus, Cline voted against raising the debt ceiling in 2023, against certifying the 2020 election results on January 6th, and against pretty much anything that might accidentally help a constituent who isn't already incorporated.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Cline is a case study in populist theater. He will rail against government waste in press releases from his Harrisonburg office, but when it comes to actually auditing the Pentagon — the single largest line item in discretionary spending — the silence is deafening. The Defense Department has failed every single audit since Congress required them in 2018, hemorrhaging trillions in unaccounted spending, and Cline, who sits on the Appropriations Committee with direct oversight power, has treated this fact with the enthusiasm of a man asked to audit his own expense account.

Corporate PAC money flows into Cline's campaign from the usual suspects: real estate, insurance, banking, and the broader financial services industry that benefits handsomely from the tax loopholes and deregulation he champions. His OpenSecrets profile reads like a lobbying firm's client list. Yet Cline brands himself as a man of the people, a small-government conservative fighting the swamp — while cashing checks from the very swamp creatures he pretends to oppose.

On banning congressional stock trading, Cline has been conspicuously quiet. On dark money disclosure, his voting record aligns with the Republican leadership position of treating anonymous campaign spending as a form of free speech. The DISCLOSE Act? Dead on arrival with members like Cline. Term limits? He will tell constituents he supports them at town halls in the Valley, but sponsoring actual legislation is another matter entirely. Paid family leave, junk fee bans, right to repair — these kitchen-table issues that poll at 80 percent or higher among Americans get the same treatment from Cline: a shrug dressed up as a principled stand for limited government.

The most revealing moment in Cline's record may be January 6, 2021, when — hours after a violent mob stormed the Capitol — he voted to reject the electoral votes from Arizona and Pennsylvania. He then condemned the violence while rewarding its purpose. That is the Ben Cline formula in miniature: say the right words, vote the wrong way, and hope the folks back in Staunton are too busy to notice the difference.

Angie Craig spent over a decade climbing the corporate ladder at St. Jude Medical, eventually becoming a vice president before the company was swallowed by Abbott Laboratories in 2017 — a career trajectory that, in most congressional districts, would make her a Republican donor rather than a Democratic candidate [1]. Instead, Craig took her Fortune 500 rolodex and ran for Minnesota's 2nd District, losing narrowly in 2016 before flipping the suburban swing seat in 2018. She has held it ever since through a combination of relentless constituent service and the kind of militant centrism that makes ideological purists on both sides quietly furious. She is the first openly LGBT member of Congress from Minnesota and the first lesbian mother to serve in the House, milestones she has worn lightly while focusing on kitchen-table issues like insulin prices and fentanyl interdiction [2].

On the 80% Bill's core planks, Craig has been one of the more active champions in the House. She has made banning congressional stock trading a signature crusade, introducing her own bill requiring full divestment and leading a bipartisan coalition of twenty-six members to pressure House leadership for a floor vote — a request that leadership has, with impressive consistency, declined to honor [3]. Her HUMBLE Act would also ban members from serving on corporate boards, eliminate taxpayer-funded first-class flights, and kill automatic pay raises for Congress, a package of reforms so obviously sensible that its failure to advance tells you everything about the institution. She co-sponsored the For the People Act's campaign finance overhaul and has backed the DISCLOSE Act's donor transparency requirements, positions that put her squarely in the 80% Bill's good graces on anti-corruption measures [4].

Healthcare is where Craig's corporate background actually proves useful rather than disqualifying. She introduced the Affordable Insulin Now Act to cap copays at thirty-five dollars a month and has pushed to extend that cap beyond Medicare recipients to Americans with private insurance [5]. She has championed Medicare drug price negotiation and authored legislation to close the orphan drug loophole that pharmaceutical companies exploit to shield blockbuster drugs from competition. For someone who spent years in the medical device industry, she has shown a notable willingness to bite the hand that once fed her — or at least to nibble on its fingers with bipartisan legislation [6].

Craig's record on the remaining planks is solid if unsurprising for a moderate Democrat representing a district that stretches from Minneapolis suburbs to rural farmland. She has supported paid family leave, Social Security expansion, and protections for American farmland from foreign acquisition. She backed the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and the CHIPS Act, and has positioned herself as a border-security hawk relative to her party, co-leading efforts on fentanyl interdiction [7]. In April 2025, she announced a Senate bid to succeed Tina Smith, a move that will test whether the pragmatic, reform-minded brand she built in MN-2 can scale statewide. Her alignment with the 80% Bill's twenty planks is not born of progressive zeal but of a suburban legislator's hard-nosed calculation that popular policies should, in theory, be passable — a premise that Congress continues to treat as charmingly naive [8].

Dan Crenshaw arrived in Congress in 2019 as a decorated Navy SEAL with an eyepatch and a bestselling book, and spent the next seven years proving that military valor and legislative integrity are entirely separate skill sets. The Republican from Texas's 2nd district built a media empire on tough-guy branding while quietly violating the very financial transparency laws he was supposed to uphold — and then lost his 2026 primary to a state representative from the far-right flank, a fitting end to a career built on style over substance.

On congressional stock trading, Crenshaw achieved the rare feat of being on every side of the issue simultaneously. He co-sponsored the Stop Insider Trading Act to ban individual stock trades by members of Congress, then went on a profanity-laced rant when asked if he would actually vote for a trading ban, calling it "number 1,000 on my priority list." He dismissed both stock trading bans and term limits in one breath: "All my problems will be solved if there's no stock trading; all my problems will be solved if there's term limits — but if you think about these things for more than five whole minutes, you might come to a different conclusion." The voters of TX-2 apparently did think about it, and concluded Crenshaw was the problem.

Then there is the matter of his own trades. In March 2020, as the pandemic crashed global markets and Congress debated the CARES Act, Crenshaw went on a buying spree — snapping up shares in Amazon, Boeing, Southwest Airlines, SPX, and Kinder Morgan. He failed to disclose these purchases as required by the STOCK Act, omitted them from his annual filing, and only came clean in an amended report filed nine months later. Boeing's employee PAC had donated $3,000 to his campaign. The oil and gas industry — his largest donor bloc — poured $453,247 into his 2020 reelection, with $10,000 checks from Energy Transfer, Valero, Occidental Petroleum, and Marathon Petroleum. He then pushed for oil industry bailouts during the pandemic. Coincidence, surely.

Crenshaw campaigned in 2018 on letting Medicare negotiate drug prices — a bold, popular position. By 2019, he had quietly abandoned it, retreating to safe Republican orthodoxy. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which actually implemented Medicare drug negotiation, calling it a bill with "about zero chance" of reducing inflation. The negotiated prices went into effect anyway, saving seniors billions.

A congressman who violated his own disclosure requirements, abandoned his campaign promises, dismissed the reforms 80 percent of Americans support, and cashed checks from every corporate PAC in Houston. Texas voters finally saw through the eyepatch.

Jason Crow is a Democrat representing Colorado's 6th Congressional District, an Army Ranger who served three combat tours in Iraq and Afghanistan before flipping a Republican-held suburban Denver seat in 2018 [1]. He sits on the House Armed Services Committee and the House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence, and co-chairs the End Corruption Caucus [2]. His biography is the kind that makes campaign consultants weep with joy: Bronze Star recipient, former litigator, father of two, member of the Congressional Dads Caucus. He has never taken a dime of corporate PAC money — a claim he repeats often enough that it functions as both badge of honor and preemptive defense [3].

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Crow is one of the more aligned members of Congress. He co-sponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading through the TRUST in Congress Act [4]. He introduced the Limit on the Expansion of the AUMF Act to reassert congressional war powers, declaring that "we must prevent another endless war" — a striking statement from a combat veteran on the Armed Services Committee [5]. He has earned an A+ rating from End Citizens United for his work fighting dark money, introduced the End Dark Money Act, and is a longtime sponsor of the constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United [6][7]. He supports protecting and strengthening Social Security and Medicare, earning a 100 percent rating from the National Committee to Preserve Social Security and Medicare [8]. He voted for the Build Back Better Act's provisions on paid family leave and prescription drug negotiation [9]. He pushed for campaign finance transparency through his ETHICS Act targeting pop-up PACs [10].

Where Crow gets complicated is the space between his reform credentials and his defense-world fluency. As Ranking Member on the Armed Services Subcommittee on Intelligence and Special Operations, he is embedded in exactly the military-industrial ecosystem that the 80% Bill's Pentagon audit and AUMF repeal provisions target [2]. He has acknowledged the Pentagon has never passed an audit and questioned why the U.S. allowed a twenty-year war in Afghanistan to continue when "there were more than enough signs that this war was unwinnable," yet his committee work centers on sustaining defense capabilities rather than fundamentally restructuring military spending [11]. He walks a careful line — critical enough to maintain credibility, supportive enough to keep the defense establishment comfortable.

Then there is the money. Crow refuses corporate PAC donations, but individual donors from defense and tech firms have found their way to his coffers. Palantir executives — including CEO Alex Karp, CTO Shyam Sankar, and head of government affairs Mehdi Alhassani — contributed $54,625 to his campaigns since 2021, making the AI surveillance company his top contributor in 2024 [12]. Palantir builds targeting technology for the Israel Defense Forces and surveillance platforms for ICE, which created an acute political problem when protesters disrupted a Crow town hall over those ties [13]. To his credit, Crow stopped accepting Palantir-linked donations in March 2025 and announced he would redirect the funds to immigrant rights organizations — a response that acknowledged the contradiction even if it did not erase it [14].

The bottom line: Jason Crow checks more boxes on the 80% Bill than most of his colleagues in either party. He has put real legislative effort behind banning stock trading, ending dark money, reforming war powers, and protecting Social Security. But his perch on the Armed Services Committee and the Palantir episode reveal the gravitational pull that defense and surveillance money exerts even on members who swear off corporate PACs. The question for CO-6 voters is whether Crow's reform instincts will continue to outpace the institutional pressures that come with being a national-security Democrat in a district that borders some of the largest defense installations in the country.

Gil Cisneros has one of the more unusual origin stories in Congress. In 2010, the Navy veteran and former Frito-Lay manager walked into a Hawaiian barbecue restaurant in Pico Rivera two weeks after being laid off and bought a Mega Millions ticket that won him $266 million [1]. He and his wife turned their windfall into a philanthropic operation, founding scholarships for Latino students at USC and George Washington University and establishing a $20 million foundation focused on education [2]. He then spent roughly $8 million of his own money to win California's 39th congressional district in 2018 [3], lost it in 2020, served as Under Secretary of Defense for Personnel and Readiness under Biden [4], and returned to Congress in 2025 representing the 31st district. It is, in a sense, the American dream with a political sequel -- lottery winner turned philanthropist turned congressman turned Pentagon official turned congressman again.

On the 80% Bill's reform agenda, Cisneros talks a strong game and has some receipts to show for it. During his first term, he cosponsored H.R. 1, the For the People Act -- the sweeping anti-corruption bill that addressed campaign finance reform, dark money disclosure, gerrymandering, and voter empowerment [5]. He tweeted about it on the anniversary of Citizens United, pledging to overturn the decision with a constitutional amendment [6]. His campaign website states he supports the DISCLOSE Act, refuses donations from Big Pharma, oil corporations, tobacco, gun manufacturers, and private prisons, and wants to limit lobbyist influence [7]. He voted for the Inflation Reduction Act's predecessor, the Build Back Better Act, which contained Medicare drug price negotiation and paid family leave provisions. On Armed Services and Veterans Affairs, where he served during his first term, he was positioned to push for Pentagon accountability, though his legislative output focused more on military personnel issues like the Brandon Act and defense health services than on the structural auditing reforms the 80% Bill envisions [8].

The corporate PAC story deserves its own paragraph because it perfectly illustrates the gap between campaign rhetoric and campaign finance reality. Cisneros pledged in 2018 to reject all corporate PAC money -- a clean, populist promise [9]. Federal records, however, showed that while he returned money sent directly from corporate PACs, he accepted at least $22,000 from other candidates' committees that were themselves partly funded by corporate PAC dollars [10]. It is the political equivalent of saying you do not eat sugar while drinking juice from concentrate. The Voice of OC documented this pattern across multiple Democratic candidates in the contested Orange County races, and Cisneros's campaign did not dispute the indirect flow of corporate PAC money [10]. For his 2024 comeback campaign, he raised $6.1 million while again claiming independence from corporate interests [11]. His OpenSecrets profile tracks the usual mix of individual and PAC contributions that come with being a well-funded Democrat in a competitive California district [12].

What makes Cisneros genuinely unusual is the self-funding capacity. A man worth hundreds of millions from a lottery jackpot does not need corporate PAC money, lobbyist bundlers, or pharmaceutical industry largesse. He could, in theory, be the most independent voice in the House -- free to champion every plank of the 80% Bill without worrying about donor backlash. That he still plays the standard fundraising game, complete with the indirect corporate PAC workarounds, suggests that the gravitational pull of Washington's money culture is strong enough to capture even someone who literally won the lottery. His policy positions on campaign finance, dark money, and Citizens United are directionally correct. The question is whether a multimillionaire who pledges to reject corporate money while still accepting its laundered cousins is the reformer voters think they are getting, or just another member of the club with better talking points and a luckier Mega Millions ticket.

Troy A. Carter, the Democratic congressman representing Louisiana's 2nd District, is the kind of politician who has never met a historic first he could not claim. First African American to represent District 102 in the Louisiana House, first Black member of New Orleans City Council District C, first African American to serve as Louisiana Senate Minority Leader -- and now, representative of a district stretching from New Orleans to Baton Rouge that includes some of the most polluted industrial corridors in America [1]. Carter sits on the powerful Energy and Commerce Committee as well as Homeland Security, which gives him a seat at the table on everything from drug pricing to pipeline permitting.

On the 80% Bill issues that track with Democratic orthodoxy, Carter is generally where you would expect. He voted for the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, which included body camera mandates for federal officers, and hosted listening sessions with families of police violence victims to push for accountability reforms [2]. He supported the Inflation Reduction Act, which contained the first-ever Medicare drug price negotiation provisions. As a Congressional Black Caucus member, he has backed efforts to fund Social Security, expand paid family leave, and close dark money loopholes through the DISCLOSE Act [3].

The complications arrive at the intersection of Carter's district and its dominant industry. During his 2021 special election campaign, Carter signed the No Fossil Fuel Money Pledge -- and then promptly accepted at least $19,000 from oil and gas interests, including donations from Entergy executives and $7,200 from liquefied natural gas company Venture Global LNG [4]. His seat on the Energy and Commerce Committee makes him a magnet for energy industry lobbying, and Carter has rewarded the attention by championing a $300 million grid resilience investment funded by the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, a project that directly benefits Entergy New Orleans [5]. On banning corporate PACs or seriously taxing the ultra-wealthy, Carter's enthusiasm dims to a polite silence.

Follow the money and the pattern sharpens. Carter's top donor industries read like a who's who of sectors with business before the Energy and Commerce Committee. His leadership PAC, Build The Bench, spreads modest sums to fellow Democrats while his campaign committee hoovers up contributions from the very industries his committee oversees [6]. For a congressman whose district includes Cancer Alley -- the 85-mile stretch of petrochemical plants between New Orleans and Baton Rouge -- the coziness with fossil fuel donors is not merely hypocritical; it is a direct conflict of interest with the health of his own constituents.

The bottom line on Troy Carter is that he is a reliable Democratic vote on the headline items -- policing reform, drug pricing, Social Security -- but an unreliable champion where industry money meets popular will. He signed a no-fossil-fuel pledge and broke it within weeks. He represents communities choking on industrial pollution and takes checks from the polluters. The 80% Bill asks whether your representative fights for what voters actually want; Carter fights for it, except when the check clears first.
